ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general paratha entry; no specific tandoor-cooked paratha subtype exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific paratha or tandoor paratha code; 'Flat bread' (A00LC) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for tandoor paratha or paratha flatbread at the time of processing.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium tandoor paratha weighing approximately 90 g, as typically served at an Indian restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Paratha, plain — Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, ICMR-NIN
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports plain paratha (tawa-cooked) per 100 g. Tandoor paratha uses similar dough composition but is baked in a clay oven (tandoor) with less added fat than tawa paratha. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN plain paratha data, with fat slightly reduced to reflect tandoor cooking method (less surface ghee applied). Energy recalculated accordingly. This is the highest-priority approved source for Indian dishes.

gi_source.title: Chapati / Indian flatbread GI values — University of Sydney GI Database; supplemented by Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load, Am J Clin Nutr
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for tandoor paratha exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The closest analogous food is Indian wheat flatbread (chapati/roti), which has a reported GI of approximately 52–72 depending on flour type and cooking method. Tandoor paratha uses refined and whole wheat flour blend and is baked at high heat, producing a slightly higher GI than plain chapati. A GI of 62 (medium) is used as a defensible estimate, consistent with Atkinson et al. (2021) values for Indian wheat flatbreads cooked at high temperature. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (62 × 42.5) / 100 ≈ 26.4, rounded to 26.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g; ~288 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit tandoor paratha to one piece per meal and avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or salted butter to keep total sodium within the recommended 2,000 mg/day limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~62) and moderate glycemic load (~26.5 per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume tandoor paratha in controlled portions (half to one piece) and pair it with high-fibre vegetables or legumes to blunt the postprandial glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee (~3.2 g per 100 g) and refined flour content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit tandoor paratha to one piece per meal, request minimal ghee application, and favour whole wheat versions to reduce saturated fat and refined carbohydrate intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tandoor Paratha is an Indian layered flatbread baked in a traditional clay oven called a tandoor, which reaches temperatures of 400–480°C. The dough is made from whole wheat flour (atta) or a blend of whole wheat and refined flour (maida), mixed with water, salt, and a small amount of ghee or oil. The dough is rolled into thin layers, folded repeatedly to create a flaky, laminated texture, and then slapped onto the inner wall of the tandoor to cook. The result is a slightly charred, crisp exterior with a soft, layered interior and a mild, nutty, wheaty flavour. Tandoor paratha differs from tawa paratha (cooked on a flat griddle) in that the tandoor imparts a distinctive smoky char and requires less surface fat. It is a staple accompaniment to Indian curries, dal, paneer dishes, and grilled meats. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 297 kcal, 7.8 g protein, 8.5 g fat, and 47.2 g carbohydrate with 2.8 g dietary fibre. The glycemic index is estimated at 62 (medium), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content (~320 mg/100 g) warrants attention for those with hypertension. In Singapore, tandoor paratha is commonly served at North Indian restaurants as a bread course alongside curries and is priced around SGD 7 per piece.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a tandoor paratha?
Is tandoor paratha good for diabetics?
What is the difference between tandoor paratha and tawa paratha?
What is the GI of paratha?
Can I eat paratha if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN entry for tandoor paratha exists; values are adapted from ICMR-NIN plain paratha data with fat adjusted downward to reflect tandoor cooking. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database values for Indian wheat flatbreads. Confidence is medium rather than high due to these adaptations.
notes: Nutrient values adapted from ICMR-NIN plain paratha (tawa) per 100 g, with fat_g reduced from ~11 g to ~8.5 g to reflect tandoor cooking method (less surface ghee). Energy recalculated using Atwater factors. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN paratha entry. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN paratha entry. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN paratha entry (paratha is a plant-based bread product with negligible B12). folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN paratha entry. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN paratha entry. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN paratha entry. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN paratha entry. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 as paratha is a baked wheat product containing no vitamin C; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN data for wheat flatbreads. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for Indian wheat flatbreads (chapati/roti range 52–72); tandoor high-heat baking supports mid-range estimate. GL per serving = (62 × 42.5) / 100 ≈ 26.5 (serving size 90 g, carbohydrate per serving ≈ 42.5 g). Dietary flags: is_vegan set to false because ghee (clarified butter) is typically used; is_dairy_free set to false for the same reason. Halal status assumed based on typical ingredients; individual restaurant preparation should be verified. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03302397 is the closest parent for paratha flatbread; no tandoor-specific subtype exists in FoodOn. FoodEx2 code A00LC (Flat bread) is the closest parent; no paratha-specific code exists in FoodEx2 at time of processing.

improvement_actions_taken: Populated all mandatory multilingual local_names for Indian cuisine in Singapore context (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi).
Adapted ICMR-NIN plain paratha nutrient values for tandoor cooking method with documented rationale.
Documented all -1 sentinel values with explicit reasons in notes field.
Documented vitamin_c_mg = 0.0 as confirmed zero for wheat flatbread.
Used University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for GI with full documentation.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases are perfectly consistent.
Applied GI category threshold correctly: GI 62 falls in 56–69 range = medium.
Used match_type values exclusively from allowed set: exact, closest_parent, none.
Included Type 2 Diabetes with correct human_condition_name and machine key.
